prestart.reminder: Press Play (▶) in the Floating Window when you're ready to begin. Once the test has started, do not press Pause (⏸) until you finish reading: Pause ends the test; it does not pause the timer. Press Stop/Reset (⏹) only if you want to cancel the test.

entry.title: Reading speed test
entry.intro:
This test is meant to estimate your real reading speed through a guided session. The app will open the text and wait for you to press Play (▶) in the Floating Window when you're ready to begin. Once the test has started, do not press Pause (⏸) until you finish reading: Pause ends the test; it does not pause the timer. Use Stop/Reset (⏹) only if you want to cancel the test. Afterwards, you may review comprehension questions if the text includes them and, at the end, create a preset from the result.
Before you begin, keep in mind that these test texts usually require between 5 and 15 minutes of uninterrupted concentration.
This pool uses short texts, so treat the result with caution. It should not be directly extrapolated to long texts, where positive factors such as flow and contextual buildup, and negative factors such as mental recapitulation and fatigue, usually come into play.
entry.pool_exhausted_message: There are no remaining unused test files. Reset the pool or add more files.
entry.visible_empty_bundled_hidden_message: Built-in test files are currently hidden, and no visible unused test files remain. Show the built-in files again or import more files to continue.
entry.start_random_confirm: This will overwrite the current text with a random eligible text from the pool. Do you want to continue?
entry.reset_confirm: This will reset the whole test pool and mark all of its files as unused again. Do you want to continue?

questions.title: Reading Comprehension Questions
questions.intro: Answering these reading comprehension questions is optional and does not affect your final score. They are only meant to help you assess the text's difficulty and whether your reading pace and style allowed you to reach a basic level of comprehension. Your answers are not saved or sent to the developers or to anyone else.
questions.incomplete_warning: All questions must be answered before evaluating.
questions.chance_at_least_observed: Chance of getting at least this score by random guessing: {percentage}

tips.tip1: Precise mode uses Unicode segmentation to count characters (graphemes) and words using 'Intl.Segmenter'.
tips.tip2: When you manually enter a time in the stopwatch, invisible fractions of a second are removed.
tips.tip3: To select all the text in an area, use Ctrl + A (Windows and Linux) or Command(⌘) + A (Mac).
tips.tip4: You can select text by first selecting the text at one end and then, while holding Shift, clicking the other end.
tips.tip5: A standard book usually contains between 250 and 300 words per page (this only applies to languages that use the Latin alphabet, or closely related derivatives, and have a similar grammatical structure).
tips.tip6: 'What you have found, then, is not a drug of wisdom and memory, but a mere reminder.' (Plato)
tips.tip7: 'Written speech requires a double abstraction: from the sound aspect of language and from the interlocutor.' (Vygotsky)
tips.tip8: toT is a primarily local app: it does not collect telemetry or usage metrics, and the text you type, paste, edit, or process is not sent to the developer's servers.
tips.tip9: Snapshots are local files: they let you save and recover working texts without depending on a cloud.
tips.tip10: The reading speed test does not send your WPM or your comprehension answers to the Internet; its result is used locally.
tips.tip11: After exporting the text extracted by OCR, the app tries to delete the temporary document created in Google for that conversion.
tips.tip12: The current text can contain up to 50,000,000 characters: the limit exists to prevent freezes, excessive payloads, and extreme memory use.
tips.tip13: toT uses the system's external browser for authorizations and sensitive links, avoiding embedded flows inside the app.
tips.tip14: The reading speed test result can create a preset, but it does not represent a universal speed: different types of text require different rhythms.
tips.tip15: OCR extraction can take seconds or minutes, depending on the size and complexity of the file.
tips.tip16: If a PDF has selectable text, native extraction is usually faster than OCR, but the quality of the result may vary.
tips.tip17: Default presets can be restored: you can experiment with custom speeds without losing a reference baseline.
tips.tip18: OCR usually works better with horizontal, focused text and without strong shadows, stains, complex backgrounds, or excessive tilt.
tips.tip19: In the reading speed test, answering the comprehension questions is optional and does not affect your final score.
tips.tip20: Reading speed is a performance indicator, but it does not necessarily reflect text comprehension.
tips.tip21: You can use the reading speed test to evaluate your progress with different types of text and adjust your presets accordingly.
tips.tip22: If you find a bug or have a suggestion, you can report it through GitHub Issues or write to the project's contact email.
tips.tip23: The tasks feature lets you organize multiple texts with estimated reading times, links, and comments.
tips.tip24: In the task editor, you can save individual rows to a library and reuse them in different tasks.
tips.tip25: toT is an open-source project. If you want to contribute, check the repository on GitHub and feel free to open an issue or a pull request.
tips.tip26: toT distinguishes between native extraction and OCR because not all documents contain selectable text, even if they visually look like text.
tips.tip27: In the task editor, columns can be resized to adapt the table to the type of information you are organizing.
tips.tip28: The Floating Window lets you keep following the stopwatch while you read in another window or application.
tips.tip29: If you want to use your own text for the reading speed test, load it as the current text and then start the test with the corresponding option.
tips.tip30: Spoiler mode hides the final segment of the preview so you can estimate or prepare a reading without accidentally seeing the ending of the text.
tips.tip31: When the text editor is maximized, the side gutters help control the real reading width. To restore the default width, double-click the gutter.
tips.tip32: In the text editor, you can find and replace text using Ctrl + F and Ctrl + H (Windows and Linux) or ⌘ + F and ⌘ + ⌥ + F (Mac).
tips.tip33: In the text editor, the CALC/SAVE button updates the current text and recalculates the count results. If Auto is enabled, this action is performed automatically.
tips.tip34: The editor can use spellcheck according to the app's active language, when the system has the corresponding dictionary available.
tips.tip35: In the editor, text search is case-insensitive and accent-insensitive. However, replacement does respect accents.
tips.tip36: Adding from the clipboard preserves the current text; replacing from the clipboard substitutes it completely.
tips.tip37: Text snapshot tags are optional, but they can be used to classify them. For example, the reading speed test uses them to filter texts.
tips.tip38: In the task editor, links can be URLs or paths to local files. When you click the link button, the app will try to open it.
tips.tip39: The stopwatch calculates real WPM by comparing the measured time with the number of words in the current text.
tips.tip40: If you change the current text during a session, it is advisable to reset the stopwatch before interpreting the resulting speed.
tips.tip41: OCR extraction temporarily uses Google Drive to convert the selected file and return the extracted text to the app.
tips.tip42: Short test texts are useful for calibration, but a speed obtained in a few minutes does not always extrapolate well to long readings.
tips.tip43: Tasks are designed to plan reading loads; snapshots are designed to preserve texts.
tips.tip44: The current text preview replaces line breaks with spaces to show a compact view in the main window.
tips.tip45: When the reading speed test uses a text from the pool, that file is marked as used to prevent future repetitions until you reset the pool.
tips.tip46: If you save a text snapshot in the 'reading_speed_test_pool' folder, the reading speed test will be able to use it as a test text.
tips.tip47: The test pool importer can incorporate individual test files or compressed packages, depending on the format you use.
tips.tip48: OCR stands for Optical Character Recognition and is a technology that converts images of text into editable and selectable text.
tips.tip49: One of OCR's predecessors was the optophone, an early 20th-century machine designed so blind people could convert printed text into sounds.
tips.tip50: The development of modern OCR accelerated between the 1950s and 1960s, driven by postal automation and the reading of business documents.
tips.tip51: Kurzweil's reading machine, introduced in the 1970s, combined a scanner, OCR, and speech synthesis to read printed text aloud.
tips.tip52: A typical OCR error is confusing visually similar characters, such as O and 0, l and I, or rn and m.
tips.tip53: OCR has evolved with machine learning techniques and neural networks, improving its accuracy and ability to handle different types of text and images.
tips.tip54: Have you tried toT's browser extensions?
